coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dot cross-linked carboxymethyl cellulose nanocomposite hydrogel for pH-sensitive oral anticancer drug delivery with potential bioimaging properties
coredata.dc:creator: Rakhshaei, Rasul
Namazi, Hassan
Hamishehkar, Hamed
Rahimi, Mahdi
coredata.dc:description: Herein, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were introduced as a novel and safe crosslinker for carboxymethyl cellulose to make biodegradable and biocompatible hydrogels. The casting was used as a simple method for the preparation of the CMC/GQDs films. Effects of the GQDs percentage on the physicochemical properties of the films were studied, and several characterizations were performed including Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, UV–vis spectroscopy, scanning electron microscopy, gas permeability, and mechanical testing analysis. The CMC/GQDs showed a pH-sensitive swelling and degradation with improved tensile strength. Fluorescent properties were also studied to evaluate the potential of the prepared CMC/GQDs nanocomposite for fluorescent bioimaging applications. Drug delivery property of the CMC-GQDs were studied using doxorubicin (DOX) as a model anticancer drug. Cytotoxicity studies were carried out using human colon adenocarcinoma HT29 cells. The prepared CMC/GQDs exhibited biocompatibility and pH-sensitive drug delivery behavior which proposed the prepared nanocomposite hydrogel has the potential to be used as a pH-triggered site-specific drug delivery system.
coredata.dcterms:subject: pH-sensitive hydrogel bionanocomposite
Graphene quantum dot
Anticancer drug delivery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: MTT assay and cell nuclei morphology studies

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors gratefully acknowledge the University of Tabriz (Grant Number 938645702 ) and RCPN of the Tabriz University of Medical Science for the financial support for this research. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of GQDs: DLS spectrum (a), photographs of the optical properties of a dilute aqueous solution of GQDs under day and 365 nm UV light (b), Photoluminescence spectra of the dilute aqueous solution of GQDs (c). Photograph of CMC/GQDs films containing 0%, 15%, 30%, and 45% GQDs under visible 365 nm UV light (d), FT-IR spectra for the GQDs, pure CMC, CMC/GQDs (e).
Possible reaction mechanism of CMC with graphene quantum dots.
SEM images of pure CMC (a) and CMC/GQDs films containing 15% (b), 30% (c), and 45% (d) GQD.
Swelling ratios at pH 2 (a) and 7.4 (b) and biodegradation at pH 2 (c) and 7.4 (d) of the CMC/GQDs hydrogel films containing 15%, 30%, and 45% GQDs during two days.
The effect of GQDs content on the water vapor permeability (WVP) (a), tensile (b), and elongation at break (c) of the CMC/GQDs films.
DOX loading percentage after 5 and 24 h (a), photograph of the DOX loaded CMC/GQDs (b), and DOX release of the prepared CMC/GQDs containing 30% (c) and 45% (d) GQDs in pH 7.4 and 2.
(a) Cell viability studies using HT29 cells, (b) DAPI stained nuclei of colon cancer cells (HT29 cells) with CMC/GQDs at various GQDs percentage (15%, 30%, and 45%).
Mass and DOX loading data for each sample.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dot cross-linked carboxymethyl cellulose nanocomposite hydrogel for pH-sensitive oral anticancer drug delivery with potential bioimaging properties

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Nanopolymers, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, P.O. Box 51666, Tabriz, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Nanopolymers, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, P.O. Box 51666, Tabriz, Iran.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Nanopolymers

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were introduced as a novel and safe crosslinker for carboxymethyl cellulose to make biodegradable and biocompatible hydrogels. The casting was used as a simple method for the preparation of the CMC/GQDs films. Effects of the GQDs percentage on the physicochemical properties of the films were studied, and several characterizations were performed including Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, UV–vis spectroscopy, scanning electron microscopy, gas permeability, and mechanical testing analysis. The CMC/GQDs showed a pH-sensitive swelling and degradation with improved tensile strength. Fluorescent properties were also studied to evaluate the potential of the prepared CMC/GQDs nanocomposite for fluorescent bioimaging applications. Drug delivery property of the CMC-GQDs were studied using doxorubicin (DOX) as a model anticancer drug. Cytotoxicity studies were carried out using human colon adenocarcinoma HT29 cells. The prepared CMC/GQDs exhibited biocompatibility and pH-sensitive drug delivery behavior which proposed the prepared nanocomposite hydrogel has the potential to be used as a pH-triggered site-specific drug delivery system.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogels, 3D cross-linked highly hydrated networks, resemble physicochemical and biological properties of extracellular matrix [1–7]. They can swell several times their weight, mimicking extracellular medium, making them suitable for biomedical applications [8–12]. Moreover, the pH-sensitive hydrogels could be used in the smart site-specific and stimuli-sensitive drug delivery systems [13–15]. Sodium carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) is a carboxymethyl substituted derivative of cellulose [16,17]. CMC is biocompatible and biodegradable and has pH-sensitive swelling properties. Hence, it has a widespread application in biomedicine, especially in smart stimuli-sensitive drug delivery [18–22].

Crosslinkers are essential tools in the preparation of hydrogels. There are two main crosslinkers: chemical or physical cross-linkers. In physically crosslinked networks are based on intermolecular interactions. Whereas, in chemical crosslinking networks are prepared through covalent bonding. Generally, physical networks are temporary in comparison with chemically crosslinked networks. However, the challenge with most of the crosslinkers is the cytotoxicity and appropriate degradability [23,24]. Hence, there is a vital need to find appropriate and safe crosslinker with appropriate cytocompatibility and biodegradability.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs have appeared as promising materials for In-vivo observation of the distribution and degradation of biomaterials after implantation or injection [25–40]. Emitting fluorescence of various wavelengths, tunable luminescence, high photostability, and low toxicity make GQDs as one of the best candidates for drug delivery and bioimaging applications [41–47].

In our ongoing study about developing advanced drug delivery systems, we have used CMC as a pH-sensitive hydrogel to prepare stimuli-sensitive drug carrier for oral drug delivery. For example, our group recently prepared and studied CMC/GQDs nanocomposite hydrogel cross-linked with epichlorohydrin as a pH-sensitive drug carrier [48]. The major problem was the toxicity of the available cross-linkers like epichlorohydrin or FeCl3. In this way, we proposed citric acid and copper (II) acetate as safe cross-linkers for CMC hydrogel [49–51]. In this work, we aim to find out that could GQDs itself be used as a cross-linker for CMC or not? It is proved at the elevated temperatures, the polyfunctional carboxylic acids like citric acid cross-link cellulosic materials via esterification [52–54]. In this work, it was hypothesized that the carboxylic acid groups available on the GQDs surface could be used for crosslinking of CMC as a polyfunctional acid. Since the DOX is one of the main anticancer drugs [55–57], the ability of the CMC/GQDs to be used as a drug delivery system was studied using doxorubicin.

CMC with the degree of substitution (DS) 0.6–0.8, and viscosity 15,000 mPas/s (1% in H2O, 25 °C) was purchased from Nippon Paper Chemicals Co., Ltd., Japan. DOX was obtained from Sigma–Aldrich Co. Citric acid, glycerol, and all other materials were obtained from Merck Co and were used without further purification.

UV–vis absorption spectra of the samples were obtained using a spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, model 1700). An FTIR spectrometer (Bruker Instruments, model Aquinox 55, Germany) with KBr pellets were used to collect infrared spectra of the samples. A scanning electron microscope (SEM; LEO, 1430VP) operated at 15 kV was used to study the surface morphology of the samples. A DLS-ZP/Particle Sizer (Microtrac, model Nanotrac Wave) was used to measure the dynamic light scattering (DLS) of the nanoparticles. A spectrofluorometer (LS45, PerkinElmer) was used to record the fluorescent properties of the diluted aqueous solutions of GQDs.

The GQDs were synthesized using pyrolysis of citric acid. The citric acid (2 g) was heated to 200 °C, and the obtained orange solid was ground and dissolved in distilled water (100 mL) under vigorous stirring [58].

Predetermined amounts of GQDs were dissolved in distilled water (50 mL) to prepare 15%, 30% and 45% solutions (weight percentage of GQDs to CMC content). Then, glycerol (0.5 g) was added to this solution as a plasticizer. CMC (1 g) was dissolved in the resulted solution using magnetic stirring. The concentration of the CMC was 2% w/w. According to the previous data this is the best concentration to reach a flawless film [50].The resulted transparent and homogeneous paste (30 g) was cast to a polystyrene plate with 9 cm diameter and cured at 60 °C within 24 h. The thickness of the film was determined to be 10 µm using averaging several cross-sections of the films in SEM micrographs.

In-vitro swelling and degradation examinations were carried out at physiological temperature (37 °C) and two different pH values using phosphate-buffered saline (1 × PBS, pH = 7.4) and hydrochloric acid–potassium chloride buffer (0.1 M, pH = 2) solutions. Samples were accurately weight (Wi) and immersed in 20 mL of the buffered solutions. To determine the swelling ratio, at predetermined time intervals, samples were taken out, and surface water was removed using a filter paper and then weight (Wt). To obtain degradation, samples were oven-dried at 50 °C for two days and then weighted (Wt(d)). The following equations were used to calculate swelling ratio and degradation (The average of three measurements was reported):
(1)
Swelling
r a t i o
=
W
t
-
W
i
W
i
(2)
Degradation
=
W
i
-
W
t
d
W
i
× 100
%

To determine water vapor permeability (WVP) of the prepared nanocomposite hydrogels, films were placed in openings of glass cups with a head of 2.5 cm diameter containing distilled water (15 mL) and hermetically sealed with silicone grease. The cups were placed in a desiccator containing silica gel at 20 °C to obtain an RH gradient equal to 100%. When steady-state conditions were reached (about 2 h), the WVP was measured from the cup weight loss over 7 h using the following equation:
(3)
WVP
=
S
×
d
A
× Δ
P
where S is the slope of the weight loss vs time (g h−1), d is the film thickness (m), A is the area of exposed film (m2) and ΔP is the differential of water vapor pressure across the film (at 20 °C, ΔP = 2.33 × 103 Pa, assuming that the RH on the silica gel is negligible).

A universal testing machine (SANTAM, Model STM1, Iran) was used to determine the elongation at break and tensile strength of the samples according to the standard test method of ASTM D 882–88. SEM photographs were used to determine the thickness of the films.

To measure the drug loading capacity, 400 mg of each sample was immersed in 40 mL of a DOX solution with an initial concentration of 100 ppm for 72 h in darkness. Subsequently, drug loading was measured by UV–vis spectroscopy at 483 nm using the following formula:
(4)
Drug
loading
capasity
=
weight
of
drug
in
the
substrate
weight
of
the
substrate
× 100

In order to investigate the release of the drug and its sensitivity to pH, DOX-loaded samples were first washed with distilled water to ensure removal of the adsorbed drug. The specimens were then dried at 40 °C and immersed in 10 mL of buffer solution. Two buffer solutions with different pH were used (PBS; pH 7.4 and hydrochloric acid–potassium chloride; pH 2). The cumulative method was used to measure the released drug. For this purpose, 3 mL of the buffer solution was withdrawn at predetermined time intervals and replaced with fresh buffer solution. By measuring the absorbance of the solution by UV–vis spectroscopy at 483 nm and using the calibration curve for each drug, the concentration of the drug was determined. The drug release was calculated using the following formula:
(5)
Drug
release
=
the
amount
of
released
drug
the
amount
of
loaded
drug
× 100

Using MTT assay and the human colon adenocarcinoma HT29 cell line the in-vitro cytotoxicity of the prepared nanocomposite hydrogels was estimated [59]. The cells were cultured in RPMI 1640 medium supplemented with FBS (10%v/v), streptomycin (100 μg/mL) and penicillin (100 U/mL) in the CO2 humidified atmosphere at 37 °C. Afterwards, the cells were seeded on the sterilized samples at a seeding density of 10,000 cells per sample. Cells were treated with free DOX, CMC/GQDs (15%, 30%, and 45%), and CMC/GQDs/DOX nanocomposites at the same dosage of DOX. In this regard, an appropriate amount of composites was cut into pieces and put into the wells. After 48 h, all culture media in the wells were replaced with 180 μL fresh culture media and 20 μL of MTT solution (5 mg mL−1 in PBS) was added to each well and incubated for an additional 4 h at 37 °C. The culture media were taken out, and the purple-blue MTT formazan precipitate was dissolved in DMSO (200 μL). The absorbance values at 570 nm were determined on a multi-well plate reader (Quant Bio-tek Instruments, Winooski, VT, USA).

To sterilize samples, each sample was cut into 6 mm disks using a punching instrument and immersed in a 70% ethanol solution for 30 min. After evaporation of the ethanol, the samples were rinsed three times with PBS solution to ensure the removal of the residual ethanol. Sterilization process was completed by UV irradiation for 30 min. Subsequently, scaffolds were incubated in culture medium RPMI 1640 at 37 °C for 24 h. Culturing experiments were performed in triplicate.

DAPI staining was performed for evaluating the nuclei morphology changes (the induction of the cell of apoptosis) after treating with samples [60]. Briefly, 5 × 104 HT29 cells were seeded in the six-well plates which have 12 mm sterile coverslips. The cells allowed to attach at the bottom of each well at 37 °C for overnight. Then, the cells were treated with CMC/GQDs containing 0%, 15%, 30%, and 45% GQDs. The cells with no treatment were considered as a negative control. After incubation for 48 h, the wells were washed three times with PBS and fixed with 4% formaldehyde at ambient temperature for 20 min. After washing the cells with PBS, cells were permeabilized by adding Triton X- 100 (0.1% w/v) and incubated for 5 min. Finally, the cells were rewashed and stained by adding 300 μL of DAPI (200 ng mL−1) on the coverslips for 20 min under dark condition. The cells were evaluated under a fluorescence microscope (Olympus microscope Bh2-RFCA, Japan).

DLS experiment was used to determine the size of the synthesized particles (Fig. 1
a). Results showed that the particle size distribution is narrow and most particles are 4 to 6 nm in size. Fig. 1 b shows digital photographs of GQDs solution under visible light and UV lamp (365 nm). As can be seen, the prepared GQDs showed blue emission under 365 nm UV radiation. Photoluminescent spectroscopy ( Fig. 1 c) was used to characterize the photo-activity of the prepared nanocomposite hydrogel to probe the potential for bioimaging applications. The maximum UV excitation and emission of the GQDs were about 367 and 461.5 nm, respectively. Fig. 1 d represents photographs of the nanocomposite hydrogel films containing different amounts of GQDs under visible light and 365 nm UV light clearly indicating the optical activity of the prepared nanocomposite films. It is evident, with increasing the GQDs concentration the intensity of the emitted blue light under 365 nm UV light was decreased. This could be due to the agglomeration of the quantum dots.

The SEM images of the pure CMC hydrogel film and CMC/GQDs nanocomposite hydrogel films are presented in Fig. 3 . A uniform and clear surface morphology are visible in the SEM photograph of the pure CMC hydrogel film (Fig. 3 a). Increase of the GQDs contents, the surface morphology of the films was changed to a rough one. As it can be seen (Fig. 3 b), nanoparticles were well dispersed in the nanocomposite hydrogel with 15% GQDs concentration. By increasing the percentage of GQDs, aggregation phenomenon can be seen on the surface of nanocomposite (Fig. 3 c and d). Better dispersion of GQDs in CMC matrix at low concentration maybe is a result of the possible H-bonding interactions between CMC and the GQDs nanoparticles. The observed aggregation phenomenon at higher concentrations can be associated with the development of the attractive natural forces between sheets of GQDs.

The results of the swelling studies are displayed in Fig. 4
a and 4b. Comparing the swelling ratio of the CMC/GQDs nanocomposite hydrogel films at pH 7.4 and 2, nanocomposites had significantly higher swelling at pH 7.4 rather than pH 2. This confirmed the prepared nanocomposite hydrogels have high pH sensitivity.

The CMC hydrogel has a maximum swelling ratio around pH 6–8. Reaching this range of pH, the carboxylic groups available on the CMC chains are deprotonated, and chains are converted to negatively charged carboxylate ions. This results in electrostatic repulsion of the CMC chains and increasing the swelling. Studying the effect of the GQDs concentration, the swelling ratio of the CMC/GQDs nanocomposite films decreased by increasing the GQDs content. This is associated with the cross-linking of the CMC chains with GQDs. This is supported by biodegradation results (Fig. 4 c and d). It was also observed by decreasing GQDs content biodegradation of nanocomposite was increased which is another evidence of the cross-linking effect of the GQDs. Using the swelling and degradation data, it could be conducted the crosslinking ratio increases with GQD content.

Gas permeability is one of the most essential characteristics of wound dressings. The influence of the GQDs content on the water vapor permeability (WVP) of the CMC/GQDs nanocomposite films is presented in Fig. 5
a. A decrease is observed in the WVP of the films with the increase of the GQDs content. This could be related to the barrier effect of the GQDs.

Fig. 5 b and 5c exhibit the elongation at break and tensile of the prepared CMC/GQDs nanocomposite films respectively. Intact CMC showed a tensile strength of 50.4 MPa. Addition of GQDs caused an increase in tensile strength up to 30%. This is related to the CMC cross-linking with GQD nanoparticles. However, a higher concentration of GQDs (45%) resulted in a decrease in tensile strength parameter. This could be related to the agglomeration of the GQDs at higher concentrations which resulted in a decrease in cross-linking degree and hence decrease of the tensile strength. The mechanical results proposed that the prepared CMC/GQDs nanocomposite films had adequate strength to be used as a dressing. Elongation at break is a parameter showing the flexibility of the samples. The addition of the GQDs resulted in a decrease of the elongation at break and flexibility. However, the flexibility of the prepared CMC/GQDs nanocomposite films was adequate for wound dressing applications.

Table 1
, Fig. 6
a and b represent DOX loading data of the samples containing 30% and 45% GQDs after 5 and 24 h loading processes and a photograph of the DOX loaded film. After 5 h, DOX loading of the nanocomposite containing 30% GQDs is approximately two times more than 45%. Decrease of the DOX loading percentage with increasing GQDs content (Fig. 6 a and b) is related to the decrease of swelling capacity with increasing GQDs contentment as a result of CMC crosslinking with GQDs. The higher swelling capacity of the sample was led to higher drug penetration and uptake. Moreover, this observation showed that the possible attraction forces between negatively charged CMC and positively charged DOX at neutral pH is more critical than π-π interaction between DOX and GQDs. The results of DOX loading confirmed that the synthesized CMC/GQDs with 45% nanoparticle could effectively be used for loading of DOX.

DOX release of the prepared CMC/GQDs containing 30% (Fig. 6 c) and 45% (Fig. 6 d) GQDs in pH 7.4 and 2. Both of the 30% and 45% GQDs containing nanocomposites were highly sensitive to pH and had a much higher drug release in pH 7.4 than 2. This sensitivity is more remarkable in CMC/GQDs 45%, which DOX release of the CMC/GQDs 45% in pH 2 is negligible. This drug release with pH-responsivity is a result of the pH-sensitive swelling of the CMC. In the simulated acidic medium of the gastric intestinal, the prepared carrier did not swell and protected the loaded drug against acidic degradation. Then, the prepared carrier enters into the duodenum and intestine with a pH value around 4–8.5. In this pH range, the results of the release experiment showed the prepared nanocomposite had the highest swelling and the drug release.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MTT assay and cell nuclei morphology studies
ce:section.ce:para:
The swelling and degradation of the samples lead to the release of the drug and degraded small molecules. The MTT experiment reflects the cytotoxicity of the released molecules. The results of the cell viability experiments are presented in Fig. 7
a. All of the CMC/GQDs nanocomposites containing 15, 30, and 45% GQDs showed cell viability over 80%. This demonstrates the cytocompatibility of the prepared CMC/GQDs nanocomposites hydrogel films. Moreover, DOX containing nanocomposite showed 21% cell viability comparable with free DOX (12%), which proved the toxicity against cancerous cell and the efficiency of the prepared carrier to be used as a smart cancer drug delivery vehicle. The lower determined toxicity of CMC/GQDs (45%)/DOX in comparison to free DOX could be related to the possible π-π attractions between DOX and GQDs.

DAPI staining was done for evaluation of the intact, apoptotic, and necrotic cells by observation of chromatin morphology [61]. It is well known that the biocompatibility of a drug carrier is the most crucial parameter for biomedical applications. To assess the toxicity and apoptotic effects of prepared nanocomposites, HT29 cells were incubated with nanocomposites for 48 h. After incubation time, the morphology of cell nuclei was visualized by fluorescent microscopy (Fig. 7 b). After treatments, the HT29 cell line which was untreated and treated with nanocomposites (three different CMC/GQDs nanocomposite films with 15%, 30%, and 45% GQDs percentages) had no changes in chromatin morphology. In general, the intensity corresponding to the cell number observed in the treated HT29 cell with CMC/GQDs at various GQDs percentages (15, 30, and 45%) was equal, which is related to the same effect of each nanocomposite to the HT29 cell line. By comparing the treated cells with control cells, there are no significant nuclei morphological changes with increasing GQDs percentage in CMC/GQDs nanocomposites.

Using a simple and low-cost casting method, a flexible and anticancer drug-eluting CMC/GQDs nanocomposite hydrogels film with controlled drug release properties were successfully prepared. GQD nanoparticles were introduced as a novel nontoxic crosslinker of CMC hydrogel with unique photoluminescent properties suitable for bioimaging applications. CMC/GQDs nanocomposite films showed a significant improvement in swelling, biodegradation, water vapor permeability, and mechanical properties. The loading and release studies revealed DOX loading improved by the increase of the GQDs content. Addition of GQDs into CMC nanocomposite hydrogel films resulted in a pH-sensitive and sustained release of DOX. According to the results, the prepared nanocomposite hydrogel films could be potentially beneficial for the pH-sensitive site-specific drug delivery systems.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts of interest to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors gratefully acknowledge the University of Tabriz (Grant Number 938645702) and RCPN of the Tabriz University of Medical Science for the financial support for this research.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2019.10.118.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
